text: The surgeon plans the port placement for a laparoscopic excision of a dermatocyst, using a ten millimeter zero degree telescope. The setup follows a baseball diamond concept with two ipsilateral ports and one contralateral port, including the telescopic port at the thumb approximation and the first working port at the left anatomical snuff box.

The surgeon inserts the Veress needle perpendicular to the skin surface, performs suction, irrigation, and the hanging drop test to confirm proper positioning for insufflation. The contact head is inserted, and gas pressures are checked, ensuring they remain in single digits until three liters of gas are inside. A hissing sound confirms the telescope's tip is correctly positioned within the abdominal cavity. The telescopic port is inserted, and a diagnostic inspection is conducted to check for any adhesions.

The surgeon inserts the first working port, followed by two ipsilateral and one contralateral port. The anterior wall of the uterus is visibly adherent to the abdominal wall, while the pouch of Douglas (POD) appears clean and unobstructed. Bipolar coagulation is used to manage omental adhesions, ensuring proper hemostasis to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon uses traumatic forceps to grasp and maneuver the ovarian cyst out of the pouch of Douglas for better exposure. Bipolar tattooing is applied to facilitate the entry of the suction cannula, which is then inserted to aspirate the cystic contents.

The surgeon exposes a clear cyst with fatty content, extending the incision with scissors to reveal the cyst wall contents. Adequate exposure allows visualization of the fatty content inside the cyst. The surgeon locates the plane between the cyst wall and the cortex, using traumatic and non-traumatic forceps, including Maryland forceps, to apply traction.

The surgeon uses Maryland forceps to peel the cyst wall, with assistance providing adequate traction. The instruments advance as the cyst wall is peeled, revealing fatty content inside. No hair, teeth, or bone are visible in the cyst wall content, and minimal or no spillage occurs during the cystectomy.

The surgeon performs a thorough wash post-cystectomy, followed by endobag extraction. Minimal oozing is observed during the cystectomy, with visible fat content within the cyst. The cortex appears thin, allowing for careful peeling from the cyst wall. As the cortex thins further, some contacts are cut to extract the cyst. The final portion of the cyst is cut with scissors, completing the integration and removal process.

The surgeon places the cyst wall inside an endobag made from a seven number glove, positioned in the anterior part of the uterus to prevent spillage into the abdominal cavity. Right and left side tubal spillage is visible. The glove's opening allows easy placement and lifting of the cyst wall, which is held with forceps. An extraction instrument is introduced through a ten millimeter port, assisted by a five millimeter scope and alligator forceps.

The surgeon uses a 10mm trocar to gently extract the cyst and its wall from the abdominal cavity, employing simple atraumatic forceps to prevent spillage. The glove is fanned over the abdominal wall to aid in extraction. After removal, the abdomen and pelvis are thoroughly washed with one liter of saline to prevent chemical peritonitis.

The surgeon injects 5 cc of ropivacaine into the five millimeter and ten millimeter ports, as well as into the abdominal cavity, followed by leaving one liter of normal saline inside the cavity to promote early recovery and reduce future adhesions.
